coredata.dc:description: Improper disposal of black liquor significantly contributes to environmental pollution in the pulping industry. While rich in organic matter, black liquor's high lignin content complicates its anaerobic digestion (AD) for biogas production. In this study, hydrothermal carbonization was applied to convert alkali lignin (AL) in black liquor into two different carbon materials, namely lignin hydrochar (LHC) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs). These materials were characterized, and their impact on AD was evaluated. The results revealed that high concentrations of AL significantly inhibited methane production, but converting AL into LHC and CQDs and adding them to the AD system at 3 g/L mitigated these inhibitory effects, enhancing biomethane production by 26.16 % and 17.91 %, respectively. Both materials improved soluble chemical oxygen demand removal, increased coenzyme F420 activity, and boosted adenosine triphosphate concentration. Moreover, electrochemical tests and excitation emission matrix analyses revealed that CQDs enhanced the conductivity and redox capability of the AD systems, while LHC promoted redox capacity and the production of humic acid-like organic compounds. Further microbial community and predicted functional gene analyses indicated that LHC and CQDs increased the abundance of microorganisms related to hydrolytic, acidifying, and direct interspecies electron transfer, enhancing three methanogenic pathways. This comprehensive study provides a strategy to convert lignin into two additives for biomethane enhancement and offers new insights into efficient black liquor bioconversion.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Improper disposal of black liquor significantly contributes to environmental pollution in the pulping industry. While rich in organic matter, black liquor's high lignin content complicates its anaerobic digestion (AD) for biogas production. In this study, hydrothermal carbonization was applied to convert alkali lignin (AL) in black liquor into two different carbon materials, namely lignin hydrochar (LHC) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs). These materials were characterized, and their impact on AD was evaluated. The results revealed that high concentrations of AL significantly inhibited methane production, but converting AL into LHC and CQDs and adding them to the AD system at 3 g/L mitigated these inhibitory effects, enhancing biomethane production by 26.16 % and 17.91 %, respectively. Both materials improved soluble chemical oxygen demand removal, increased coenzyme F420 activity, and boosted adenosine triphosphate concentration. Moreover, electrochemical tests and excitation emission matrix analyses revealed that CQDs enhanced the conductivity and redox capability of the AD systems, while LHC promoted redox capacity and the production of humic acid-like organic compounds. Further microbial community and predicted functional gene analyses indicated that LHC and CQDs increased the abundance of microorganisms related to hydrolytic, acidifying, and direct interspecies electron transfer, enhancing three methanogenic pathways. This comprehensive study provides a strategy to convert lignin into two additives for biomethane enhancement and offers new insights into efficient black liquor bioconversion.

ce:section.ce:para: Black liquor, a dark brown liquid generated from the pulping industry, has high chemical oxygen demand [1]. Direct discharge of black liquor causes serious environmental pollution problems, therefore, sustainable solutions are required to obtain benefits from the carbohydrates in black liquor [2]. Anaerobic digestion (AD) is considered to be a mature and sustainable solution to recover energy from black liquor with cost and environmental benefits [3,4]. However, the bioconversion rate of carbohydrates from black liquor is limited by lignin and low molecular weight aromatic compounds [1]. Traditionally, lignin is incinerated to generate electricity and heat, yet this approach underutilizes its potential as a precursor for valuable chemicals, materials, and fuels, given its complex and rich functional groups. Extensive research has explored both thermochemical and biological methods to depolymerize lignin and enhance its value. Thermochemical conversion techniques such as pyrolysis, gasification, and acid and base catalytic reactions yield complex mixtures of solids, liquids, and gases [5–7], while biological depolymerization methods involving bacteria, fungi, enzymes, and microbial consortia offer environmentally friendly alternatives with fewer by-products [8–11]. Despite its potential, lignin is rarely used in AD processes for methane production because it inhibits methanogen activity and is challenging to utilize in anaerobic conversion processes [12–14]. Interestingly, lignin can lead to the accumulation of short-chain fatty acids and has been reported to produce volatile fatty acids (VFAs) rather than biomethane during the anaerobic conversion of waste activated sludge [15]. Therefore, it is crucial to reduce or eliminate the inhibition effect of methanogenesis processes by lignin to achieve efficient biodegradation of black liquor.

Hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) and pyrolysis are both employed to convert lignin into useful carbon materials. HTC, in particular, is suited for moisture-rich substrates like black liquor and operates at lower temperatures compared to pyrolysis [16], leading to the formation of hydrochar with oxygen-rich functional groups [17–19]. While most research on applying HTC to methane production has focused on carbohydrates or common biomass, little research has been done on lignin-rich black liquor [20]. During the HTC process, lignin is converted into polyaromatic and phenolic hydrochar [21]. Additionally, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are also formed along with the aggregation of hydrolysis products [22]. Consequently, the lignin in black liquor is altered characteristically, and two types of lignin-based carbon materials, namely lignin hydrochar (LHC) and CQDs, can be likely obtained.

Direct interspecies electron transfer (DIET) is considered a more efficient method for energy utilization in the AD process, in which oxidizing bacteria can transfer electrons to methanogens via conductive polymers, c-type cytochromes, or conductive materials [23]. Carbon-based materials (e.g., biochar and activated carbon) have been found to accelerate methane production rates by promoting DIET [23,24]. As zero-dimensional carbon materials, CQDs possess excellent biological properties and photo/electro-chemical properties [25]. CQDs are mostly applied in bioimaging, environmental monitoring, energy development, catalyst preparation, etc [25,26]. While numerous studies have synthesized CQDs using chemicals like sodium citrate, urea, and ethylenediamine to accelerate the DIET pathway and enhance methane production [27,28], relatively few studies have utilized biomass as raw materials. For example, Liu et al. [29] synthesized CQDs from Aloe peel, enhancing methane production during co-digestion of aloe peel and dairy manure. Furthermore, with the aid of a magnetic field, CQDs increased methane production by 24 % compared to the control group [30]. Lignin, with its aromatic phenylpropane structure and rich carbon content, is particularly attractive for CQDs preparation and may have unique effects on electron transfer among microorganisms [31]. Compared to CQDs, hydrochar has received more attention for AD applications [20]. Slurry-based hydrochar has been reported to help improve acidification and methanogenic microbial activity, thereby enhancing the methanogenic process [32]. Additionally, hydrochar derived from corn straw, poplar, and rice straw has notably improved the AD performance of wastewater and solid waste [33–36]. The disposal of digestate generated during AD poses environmental challenges, and studies have shown that digestate-based hydrochar increased methane production by 49 % than the control in co-digestion [37]. However, the effects of two carbon materials (i.e., LHC and CQDs), prepared from lignin, on DIET have not been reported.

Based on these considerations, this study attempts to alleviate the inhibitory effect of lignin in black liquor on AD by using HTC and to explore the possibility of enhancing the methane production process using the two lignin-based carbon materials generated in this process. LHC and CQDs were synthesized in a stainless-steel autoclave using a one-step HTC method. Initially, diverse analytical methods were applied to characterize the physicochemical properties of LHC and CQDs. Subsequently, the methanogenic performance, biological activity, and electron transfer capacity of reactors supplemented with different concentrations of alkali lignin (AL), LHC, and CQDs were investigated, and kinetic simulations of the methanogenic process were performed. Furthermore, changes in microbial communities and key enzyme-encoding genes in anaerobic digesters with LHC and CQDs were analyzed.

Black liquor was obtained from Hainan Xiuhong Biochemical Co., Ltd., while the inoculum sludge was taken from a continuous biogas digester located in Fangshan City, Beijing, China. The inoculum had the following characteristics: total solids (TS) = 9.18 ± 0.14 %, volatile solids (VS) = 5.95 ± 0.08 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of alkali lignin from black liquor
ce:section.ce:para: The pH of the black liquor was adjusted to 12 with NaOH (0.2 mol/L) and kept at 55 °C for 3 h. The liquor was then centrifuged several times at 4000 r/min to get rid of any insoluble materials. Following this, 2 mol/L HCl was added to lower the pH to approximately 2. The adjusted liquid was stirred at 70 °C for 2 h, and then cooled and left for 24 h. The resulting mixture was centrifuged, and the pellet was washed with distilled water until the pH was close to neutral. The purified lignin was then dried at 50 °C for the whole day and ground up before use.

As shown in Fig. 1 , LHC and CQDs were synthesized through the one-pot method, using AL as the raw material. Initially, 2 g AL was dispersed in 60 mL of deionized water with sonication. The mixture was then transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. The HTC temperature was 220 °C, lasted for 10 h without any catalyst, under a pressure of about 2.3 MPa. Once the reaction was complete and the mixture had cooled to room temperature, the dark brown mixture was filtered through a 0.22 μm microfiltration hydrophilic membrane. The filtrated brown solid was washed with water until it reached neutrality, then dried under vacuum at 50 °C, and finally ground into a powder to obtain LHC. The filtrated liquor was dialyzed using a dialysis bag (1000 Da) for 48 h. The dialyzed solution was subjected to freeze-drying, resulting in the formation of CQDs powder. The conversion rates of LHC and CQDs were calculated by Eq. (1):
(1)
Conversion
rate
( % )
=
MP ML
× 100
where MP is the mass (g) of the obtained LHC or CQDs, and ML is the mass (g) of the original AL.

In this study, batch trials were conducted to assess the impact of changing concentrations of carbon additives on methane production derived from glucose. Three types of additives were tested at five different concentrations each: (1) AL at 0.1 g/L, 1 g/L, 3 g/L, 5 g/L, and 10 g/L; (2) LHC at 0.1 g/L, 1 g/L, 3 g/L, 5 g/L, and 10 g/L; and (3) CQDs at 0.1 g/L, 0.5 g/L, 1 g/L, 3 g/L, and 5 g/L.

The batch trials were carried out using 250 mL serum bottles, with each reactor containing 100 mL of inoculum and 100 mL of substrate solution to simulate a normal AD process. The substrate solution was composed of 30 g/L of glucose, 32 mg/L of KH2PO4, 400 mg/L of NH4Cl, and 400 mL/L of trace element solution. The main components of the trace element solutions were: FeSO4·7H2O (0.5 g/L), MgSO4·7H2O (62.5 mg/L), MgCl2·6H2O (62.5 mg/L), CoCl2·6H2O (12.5 mg/L), MnSO4 (12.5 mg/L), ZnSO4·7H2O (12.5 mg/L), NiCl2·6H2O (12.5 mg/L), CuSO4·5H2O (12.5 mg/L), Na2MoO4·2H2O (12.5 mg/L), and H3BO3 (12.5 mg/L) [38]. A group with only 3 g/L of AL was included to document potential organic matter biodegradation, while a blank control group (CK) without any additive was also employed. To establish anaerobic conditions, the initial pH of each bottle was adjusted to 7.0 using 1 M NaOH and 1 M HCl, followed by purging with pure N2 for 5 min. The anaerobic reactors were placed in a constant temperature room at 35 ± 1 °C for twenty days. All these tests were conducted in triplicate to ensure the reliability and accuracy of the results.

The quantification of TS, VS, and gas composition was conducted following the methods utilized in previous studies [39]. For characterizing CQDs, ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were acquired using a spectrometer (UV-2550, Shimadzu, Japan), while photoluminescence (PL) spectra were determined using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (RF-6000, Shimadzu, Japan). The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area and pore diameter of LHC and AL were measured using a surface area and pore size analyzer (Quantachrome, USA). Elemental compositions (C, N, H, O, and S) of all carbon materials were analyzed by an elemental analyzer (Elementar Vario EL III, Germany). Soluble chemical oxygen demand (sCOD) was measured using a chemical oxygen demand rapid detector (E799718, Lovibond, Germany). Additionally, the detailed morphology of CQDs was observed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20, FEI, USA), while scanning electron microscopy (SEM, S-4800, Hitachi, Japan) was employed for examining the surface morphologies of AL and LHC. The functional groups of LHC and CQDs were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, USA) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Bruker, Germany). The extraction of extracellular polymeric substances (EPS) was carried out using a heating method. Sludge (20 mL) was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min to remove the supernatant, washed twice with 15 mL of 0.05 % NaCl solution, and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min. The washed sludge was shaken in 15 mL of 0.05 % NaCl solution at 80 °C for 30 min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.45 μm membrane to extract EPS. Excitation emission matrix (EEM) fluorescence spectroscopies of EPS were conducted with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (RF-6000, Shimadzu, Japan). The electrical conductivity was determined using a resistivity tester (ST2722-SD, Suzhou Jingge Electronic Co., Ltd. China). The activity of coenzyme F420 and the content of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) in the anaerobic reactor were determined using detection kits provided by Shanghai Enzyme-linked Biotechnology Co., Ltd., according to the instructions. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) of anaerobic sludge was performed using a three-electrode configuration with a cell and electrochemical workstation (CHI760E, Chenhua, China). Platinum wire and Ag/AgCl were used as counter and reference electrodes, respectively, and 25 mL of phosphate buffer solution was used as the supporting electrolyte. Scans ranged from −1.2 to 1.0 V [40].

The modified Gompertz model (Eq. (2)) was applied to describe the kinetics of AD:
(2)
P = PM × exp
{
‐ exp
[
RM × e ×
(
λ ‐ t
)
/ PM + 1
]
}
where P represents the cumulative methane yield (mL/g VS) for digestion time t (d), PM represents the predicted maximum methane yield (mL/g VS), RM denotes the maximum methane production rate (mL/g VS/day), and λ indicates the lag time (days).

Following the AD process, the anaerobic sludge samples were extracted from each bottle and subsequently analyzed for microbial community diversity via high-throughput sequencing at Beijing Majorbio Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Polymerase chain reaction and Illumina Miseq sequencing were conducted as previously reported by Zheng et al. [41]. Phylogenetic Investigation of Communities by Reconstruction of Unobserved States (PICRUSt2) algorithm is used to predict the functional composition and gene abundance of microbial community metagenomes.

Table 1 shows the physicochemical properties and conversion rates of lignin-based carbon materials. AL exhibited a very low surface area and pore diameter, consistent with previous studies [42]. After HTC, the surface area of LHC increased, although the BET surface area remains low compared to hydrochar derived from cellulose and hemicellulose [42,43]. This is attributed to the thermally stable phenolic structure of lignin. The H, N, and O contents of LHC decreased significantly compared to AL, indicating the decomposition of these groups at high temperatures. Despite this, due to the deoxygenation reaction, carbon was preserved in large quantities, leading to an overall increase in carbon content. For CQDs, an increase in the O/C ratio was observed, and these oxygen-containing functional groups are reported to facilitate electron transfer [44]. Additionally, a significant increase in S content was noted, aligning with the findings of Zhu et al. [45]. During the HTC process, most of the AL was converted into LHC (70.18 %), while a smaller portion was converted into CQDs (9.05 %), with approximately 20 % of the material lost as incomplete small molecules. The utilization of these incomplete small molecules is the focus of further research in the future.

Fig. 2 a clearly shows the TEM image of CQDs prepared from lignin, which were spherical nanoparticles with an average diameter of 4.67 nm. The surface morphology of the AL and LHC samples are depicted in Fig. 2b and c, respectively. While the AL surface appeared to be relatively flat with only a few pores, the LHC sample exhibited a complex aggregation and agglomeration structure. It is postulated that these pores are related to the evaporation of organic matter during high temperature processes, which is in line with previous studies [46].

Subsequently, FTIR analysis was performed to elucidate the functional groups of CQDs and LHC with the aim of revealing the correlation between their constituents and the origin of AL. Fig. 2d indicated that the absorption bands of LHC and CQDs inherit some natural features of AL, including C=O (1598 cm−1), C-O (1212 cm−1), C-O-C (1134 cm−1), and C=C near 1505 cm−1. The absorption band at 3400 cm−1 was attributed to phenolic hydroxyl [47], the phenolic hydroxyl group in CQDs showed no change compared with AL, while the phenolic hydroxyl group in LHC decreased sharply. This finding is consistent with previous studies where a significant decrease in phenolic hydroxyl content was observed following HTC treatment of lignin [21,46]. In addition, the C=C peaks in LHC and CQDs have been significantly reduced, especially in CQDs. The C-H peaks attributed to lignin (2936 cm−1 and 2846 cm−1) did not change significantly in the LHC [46,47], while they nearly disappeared in the CQDs. Both LHC and CQDs feature an abundance of oxygen-containing functional groups, especially CQDs, rendering them excellent candidates for enhanced methanogenesis in AD systems.

As depicted in Fig. 3 , the C1s spectra of XPS showed three different peaks for lignin-based carbon materials, namely C-O (286.30 eV), C-C (284.80 eV), and C=O (288.76 eV) [48]. The principal functional group responsible for shaping the carbon framework in AL, CQDs, and LHC is the C-C bond, with LHC exhibiting the highest concentration of C-C bonds. Compared with AL, the C=O and C-O bonds in LHC obtained after HTC were significantly decreased, while the C=O and C-O contents of CQDs were significantly increased. Subsequently, the O1s functional group of the lignin-based carbon materials was characterized, which consisted of four main peaks, namely C=O (531.2 eV), O-H/O-C=O (532.5 eV), O=C-O, C-O (533.2 eV), and COOH (534.1 eV) [49]. The primary functional groups constituting the oxygen element in AL, CQDs, and LHC are O-H/O-C=O and O=C-O, C-O. The C=O of both CQDs and LHC obtained after HTC was increased compared to that of AL. Additionally, the relative abundance of COOH in CQDs was higher than that of AL, while the relative abundance of O-H/O-C=O decreased.

The UV–vis absorption and PL spectra were employed to investigate the optical properties of CQDs, with the results shown in Fig. 4 . The UV–visible absorption spectrum of CQDs had a π→π∗ transition shoulder at 276 nm [50]. Moreover, the inset in Fig. 4b displayed an image of the CQDs solution under visible and UV light. The CQDs solutions emitted obvious blue light under UV light, which was completely different from the color under visible light [51]. The optimal excitation wavelength for CQDs was 350 nm, and the emission wavelength was approximately 450 nm, as demonstrated in Fig. 4a and b. The emission peak of CQDs was rapidly red-shifted when the excitation wavelength was increased from 310 nm to 400 nm. The optical properties of CQDs are related to their surface defects, serving as energy storage capacitors and facilitating electron transfer during AD [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anaerobic digestion performance after adding different lignin-based additives
ce:section.ce:para: The change in cumulative methane yield is the most obvious indicator of the effect of different lignin-based carbon materials on the anaerobic conversion process. As illustrated in Fig. 5 a, methane production remained largely unaffected at concentrations of 0.1 g/L and 1 g/L of AL, while the reactor with 3 g/L AL exhibited a 7.70 % increase in methane production compared to the control group. This may be due to the small number of carbohydrates remaining in the AL, which provided part of the digestible material. Therefore, 3 g/L of AL was added to the reactor without adding substrate solution, and the batch experiments showed a methane yield was 21.73 ± 2.32 mL. After excluding this methane production, 3 g/L of AL exhibited no significant influence on the methane production of glucose. Conversely, the reactors with 5 g/L and 10 g/L AL showed a significant inhibitory effect on the anaerobic conversion of biomass, with substantial reductions in methane yield of 10.69 % and 22.59 %, respectively, relative to the control group. The presence of lignin, as demonstrated by Li et al. [14], impedes the biodegradation of cellulose and hemicellulose, leading to a dramatic decrease in methane production. Lignin can also affect enzymatic hydrolysis through non-productive adsorption by cellulases.

The HTC process significantly alters the characteristics of AL, transforming it into LHC with modified properties that affect AD performance. Fig. 5b shows that the addition of the same concentration of LHC reduced the inhibitory effect of lignin and increased the methane yield by −2.58–28.05 % compared to AL. In particular, the methane yield of the reactor with 10 g/L LHC was 255.69 ± 9.31 mL/g VS, which was not significantly different from the control, while it was 28.05 % higher than that of the reactor with 10 g/L AL. The phenolic hydroxyl group in lignin was found to be a key factor in conferring greater inhibition of enzymatic hydrolysis [52], while the decrease of phenolic hydroxyl groups in LHC may help to eliminate their negative effect in the AD process. Additionally, the reactor with 3 g/L LHC achieved the highest biomethane yield (325.42 ± 5.50 mL/g VS), which was 26.16 % and 17.14 % higher than the control and 3 g/L AL reactors, respectively.

Fig. 5c illustrates the cumulative methane yields of reactors supplemented with varied concentrations of CQDs. The methane yields of the reactors with 0.5 g/L, 1 g/L, and 3 g/L CQDs were 292.14 ± 8.90 mL/g VS, 292.75 ± 3.98 mL/g VS, and 304.15 ± 4.67 mL/g VS, respectively, which were 13.26 %, 13.50 %, and 17.91 % higher than those of the control group. Additionally, Fig. 5d shows that CQDs significantly accelerated methane production during the middle stage of AD. The methane production rate of the system with 1 g/L CQDs was 44.00 ± 2.71 mL/g VS/d on the seventh day, which was 36.26 % higher than that of the control group. Li et al. [28] added N-doped CQDs (synthesized from sodium citrate and ethylenediamine) to the AD system feed by glucose, however, no effect on methane production was observed. This suggests that CQDs derived from distinct feedstocks may have varied effects on the anaerobic conversion process, which requires further exploration.

Considering the dynamics of CH4 and CO2 levels, the examination of CH4/CO2 ratio is an important method to identify the effect of additives on biogas composition during AD [53]. As demonstrated in Fig. 6 a, increasing the concentration of AL resulted in a lower CH4/CO2 ratio. For instance, in the 10 g/L AL reactor, the CH4/CO2 ratio on the seventh day decreased by 37.19 % compared to the control. The quinone structure in lignin has been reported to compete with methanogens for electrons during AD, hindering the conversion of VFAs to methane [15]. In contrast to AL, Fig. 6b and c shows that different concentrations of LHC and CQDs increased the CH4/CO2 ratio in the middle and end stages of digestion. Notably, the 1 g/L LHC reactor reached the highest CH4/CO2 ratio on the ninth day, exceeding the control by 51.25 %.

The sCOD removal efficiency is a crucial parameter reflecting the efficiency of AD. Fig. 6d shows that low concentrations of AL had no effect on sCOD removal efficiency, while high concentrations significantly hindered it. Specifically, 10 g/L of AL reduced the sCOD removal rate by 22.59 % compared to the control. Excessive AL may weaken the ability to decompose sCOD, inhibiting the activity of microorganisms responsible for degrading organic matter. This also explains the reduction in methane yield and CH4/CO2 ratio in AL reactors. Conversely, the addition of CQDs and LHC both helped to slightly increase the sCOD removal rate. At a concentration of 5 g/L, LHC and CQDs increased the removal rate by 8.64 % and 6.80 %, respectively, compared to the control. This indicates that LHC and CQDs can accelerate the decomposition of organic matter, and the reduction of organic matter will promote methane production during the methanogenic stage.

The study employed the modified Gompertz model to simulate the methane production process and calculated various kinetic parameters, which are summarized in Table 2 . High AL concentrations (≥5 g/L) had a significant inhibitory effect on AD. In particular, both PM and RM values decreased by 21.72 % and 29.46 %, respectively, and λ values increased by 20.38 % at an AL concentration of 10 g/L. Lower PM and RM values indicate lower digestion efficiency and higher λ values indicate slower start-up [28]. Conversely, the addition of CQDs increased λ values, indicating that microorganisms in AD needed more time to acclimate to the added substances [39]. As the concentration of CQDs increased, the RM values also increased. For instance, the RM value for 3 g/L CQDs was 39.55 ± 0.61 mL/g VS/d, representing an 18.41 % increase relative to the control. Moreover, the addition of LHC increased the RM values by −2.41–38.62 % and decreased the λ values by 17.60–24.12 % compared to the corresponding concentrations of AL. Remarkably, 3 g/L LHC obtained the maximum PM and high RM values, which were 26.48 % and 26.19 % higher than the control group, respectively. Overall, the study demonstrated that HTC treatment reduced the detrimental limitation of methanogenic processes by lignin, and the obtained CQDs and LHC both promoted methane production, CH4/CO2 ratio, sCOD removal efficiency, and methane production rates.

The dissolved organic matter in the EPS was quantified using fluorescence intensity measured by EEM, with the intensity being proportional to the dissolved organic matter content. As illustrated in Fig. 7 , small amounts of fulvic acid-like organic compounds and large amounts of humic acid-like organic compounds were detected in the EPS of AD systems. Both LHC and CQDs had a considerable impact on the production of humic acid-like organics when compared to the control group, with the LHC showing a particularly pronounced effect. Humic acid-like organics are redox-active organisms that have been shown to act as electron shuttles between microorganisms for extracellular electron transfer [54,55], suggesting that both LHC and CQDs promote the DIET pathway. Conversely, the dissolved organic matter content in EPS was significantly lower in the 3 g/L AL system compared to other AD systems, indicating suppressed production of dissolved organic matter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biological activity and electron transfer system activity
ce:section.ce:para: Coenzyme F420 plays a crucial role in the key redox reactions of methanogenesis, directly influencing methanogenic activity [56]. It is also commonly used to monitor hydrogenotrophic methanogenesis activities [57]. As depicted in Fig. 8 a, the addition of 3 g/L of LHC and CQDs to the reactors resulted in an increase in coenzyme F420 activity to 47.30 ± 0.64 and 47.69 ± 0.33 IU/L, respectively, indicating active methanogen production and enhanced hydrogenotrophic methanogenic pathways. ATP, the energy currency of cells, serves as an indicator of anaerobic microbial activity and is positively correlated with electron transfer levels during anaerobic transformation [56]. Fig. 8b shows that higher ATP content was observed in the reactors with LHC and CQDs, suggesting improved microbial activity and electron transfer. Specifically, the addition of 3 g/L LHC increased ATP concentration by 38.80 % compared to the control group. Consistent with these findings, Liu et al. [27] reported an increase in ATP content upon the addition of CQDs, with further enhancement under illumination due to photoelectron generation.

The electrical conductivity of sludge, as shown in Fig. 8c, varied significantly with the addition of LHC and CQDs. LHC had minimal impact on the conductivity of the sludge, while CQDs increased the conductivity of the sludge to 0.06 μS/cm. The higher conductivity of CQDs was attributed to the formation of graphene-like structures [58]. In contrast, the electrical conductivity of hydrochar was negligible, supporting the findings of a previous study by Ren et al. [16], which reported that hydrochar prepared at 300 °C exhibited undetectable electrical conductivity. Studies indicate that the conductivity of biochar increases with the increase of pyrolysis temperature, and the biochar prepared at pyrolysis temperature lower than 300 °C has negligible conductivity [59,60]. These findings suggest a correlation between the conductivity of CQDs and DIET enhancement, while the conductivity of LHC does not appear to be associated with DIET enhancement. CV serves as a tool to characterize the electron transfer capacity of the digestion system [59], with the integrated area of the closed curve indicating the extent of charging, discharging, and electron transfer capacity, where a larger area corresponds to greater capacity [61]. As shown in Fig. 8d, the AD system with addition of LHC had the largest reduction current and integrated area, which were 45.54 % and 63.89 % higher than the control group, respectively. It was reported that surface oxygen-rich functional groups of hydrochar can facilitate redox reactions of microorganisms by providing and accepting electrons [16]. Similarly, the system with addition of CQDs displayed a 26.32 % increase in reduction current and a 45.54 % higher integrated area compared to the control group. A study similarly observed that the redox capacity of the AD system was promoted by the addition of CQDs [14]. Additionally, the system with LHC had a weak oxidation peak (0.037 V), while the sludge from the CQDs reactor had a weak reduction peak (near 0.565 V), indicating that the redox reaction for organic matter degradation was enhanced by lignin-based carbon materials [62].

The performance of AD in methane production can be influenced by the use of additives in reactors, and their effect is closely related to the microbial community. The results of alpha diversity are listed in Table 3 , with coverage indices exceeding 99.81 % for all groups, indicating that sequencing results were adequate and reliable. Both lignin-based carbon materials reduced bacterial richness and diversity, with no significant difference between them. Meanwhile, the addition of lignin-based carbon materials resulted in a significant increase in archaeal diversity, particularly in the CQDs reactor. In this specific reactor, the shannon index elevated from 0.9 to 1.16, while the simpson index decreased from 0.59 to 0.47. The increase in archaeal community diversity with the addition of CQDs and LHC suggests that they may promote the enrichment of some specific archaeal microorganisms during AD, which is essential for methane production.

Venn diagrams of operational taxonomic units (OTUs) microbial communities helped reveal differences between core/variable microorganisms in the reactor [15]. As depicted in Fig. 9 a and b, a clear crossover of OTUs between bacterial and archaeal bacteria of the reactors was found, suggesting that these microorganisms may have a key role in digestion. Both LHC and CQDs, added at concentrations of 3 g/L, led to a reduction in the number of unique bacterial OTUs in the reactors, suggesting an optimization of the bacterial community by these lignin-based carbon materials. Moreover, LHC increased the number of OTUs of archaea in the reactor, whereas CQDs did not affect the archaeal OTU count.

The relative abundance of bacteria and archaea in the microbial community of each reactor was further investigated. The distribution of bacteria at the phylum level in the three reactors is depicted in Fig. 9c, revealing a similar composition in all reactors, including Firmicutes, Chloroflexi, Bacteroidota, Synergistota, Desulfobacterota, Proteobacteria, Actinobacteriota, and others. The dominant bacteria were Firmicutes, Chloroflexi, Bacteroidetes, and Synergistota, which accounted for 86.57–90.36 % of the total. In the control group, Firmicutes constituted up to 53.16 % of the population, increasing to 57.70 % and 58.08 % in reactors with 3 g/L CQDs and LHC, respectively. Firmicutes are crucial in the decomposition of organic matter and the acidogenesis process, generating H2 and CO2 during AD, and are known for their role in electron transfer between microorganisms [62]. Yang et al. [62] observed that Firmicutes would be enriched in reactors with high DIET capacity. Furthermore, the abundance of Synergistota, important for short-chain fatty acid production [63], increased by 73.49 % (CQDs) and 9.58 % (LHC), respectively, compared to the control group. However, the addition of CQDs significantly suppressed the population of Chloroflexi, while its relative abundance was 9.50 % higher in the LHC reactor than in the control group. Chloroflexi are involved in the degradation of simple organic compounds and are known to decrease in abundance when VFAs accumulate excessively or under low pH conditions [64]. These results suggest that lignin-based carbon materials all contribute to organic matter degradation in AD. CQDs, lacking adsorption capacity, promote large-scale VFAs production by enriching Synergistota, while LHC, as a particle carbon material with adsorption capacity [36,65], promotes VFAs production by supporting both Chloroflexi and Synergistota.

As shown in Fig. 9d, the main archaea in these reactors were Methanosaeta, Methanobacterium, and Methanosarcina, occupying 92.92–97.06 % of the total population. Among them, Methanosaeta was the most crucial genus, comprising over 65.67 % of the total population in the three reactors. The strict acetoclastic methanogen Methanosaeta was slightly reduced in the reactors supplemented with these two lignin-based carbon materials. Notably, the addition of CQDs increased the relative abundance of Methanobacterium, Bathyarchaeia, and Methanosarcina by 9.66 %, 252.24 %, and 61.91 % compared to the control. Liu et al. [29] also reported an elevation in the relative abundance of Methanosarcina and Methanobacterium in the reactor with CQDs. In contrast, Methanobacterium had no significant effect in the LHC reactor, while the relative abundance of both Bathyarchaeia and Methanosarcina increased by 61.90 % and 30.70 % compared to the control. Methanosarcina is considered to be the acetoclastic methanogen and electrotropic archaea that performs DIET in the methane production process with the supplement of conductive materials [66]. Bathyarchaeia exhibits acidogenic and hydrolytic activities, while Methanobacterium is an essential hydrogenophilic methanogenic bacterium [39,67]. The substantial enrichment of Bathyarchaeia in the CQDs reactor further suggests that CQDs are more effective in promoting the hydrolysis and acidification of organic matter compared to LHC.

Although the dominant microbial community was similar, the relative abundance of key microorganisms changed significantly. The increased methane production in the AD system can be ascribed to two main factors: the active acid-producing bacteria and the enhanced DIET among methanogens. Both CQDs and LHC can promote methanogenesis by accelerating the electron transfer of microorganisms, while CQDs have a significant promotion effect on acetoclastic and hydrogenotrophic methanogenesis. Compared with CQDs, LHC had a weaker enhancement effect on microorganisms associated with DIET, but it increased microbial electron transfer by enhancing the redox capacity, ATP content, and the production of humic acid-like substances in the AD system (Figs. 7 and 8).

To reveal the main methanogenic pathways, key functional enzymes associated with the methanogenic pathway in anaerobic digesters were identified. Fig. 10 shows the major methanogenic pathways and the predicted abundance of functionally encoded genes. It clearly shows that the addition of CQDs and LHC greatly promoted the genes associated with the acetoclastic pathway, with a particularly strong effect observed in the CQDs reactor. The gene abundance of AckA and PTA in the CQDs reactor increased by a total of 61.91 % compared to the control. Additionally, EchA and MtaA, which are key enzymes in the hydrogenotrophic and methylotrophic pathways, respectively, and their gene abundance in the CQDs reactor was 1628 and 3949, respectively, higher than 935 and 2529 in controls. Similarly, the gene abundance of EchA and MtaA in the LHC reactor was 1316 and 3302, respectively, which was higher than in controls. These results suggest that lignin-based carbon materials promoted hydrogenotrophic and methylotrophic pathways. In conclusion, CQDs and LHC significantly enhanced the methanogenic process by enhancing several key genes. Nevertheless, it should be noted that the prediction of key genes using only 16S rRNA gene sequence data is still limited, and further analysis of other molecular technologies, such as metagenomic analysis, is necessary.

Black liquor, a byproduct of the pulping process, presents significant economic and environmental challenges for papermaking companies [69]. HTC technology offers a solution by converting lignin from black liquor into valuable carbon materials like LHC and CQDs. This conversion reduces environmental impacts and alleviates the economic burden on paper industries. The HTC process has been increasingly applied on a large scale, with pilot plants established in countries like Spain and Germany [70]. HTC is advantageous for processing high-moisture biomass, operating at lower temperatures, and reducing energy consumption compared to traditional thermal methods. Various biomass types including sewage sludge, sawdust, corncob, cornstalk, and rape straw can be transformed into char through HTC, recovering up to 71.6 % of energy [71]. While HTC carbon materials can be used directly as fuel and reduce carbon emissions [70], they do not offer significant economic benefits. For example, the price of HTC coal is not competitive with bituminous coal without policy support [72]. Moreover, AD of organic matter through the integration of these carbon materials enhances biogas production, which can be used for power generation, heating, or as vehicle fuel, offering a tangible economic advantage despite the lower market value of biomethane compared to natural gas. This process is further enhanced by environmental taxes on fossil fuels, making it more competitive. AD also significantly reduces greenhouse gas emissions compared to incineration or landfilling and produces digestate that can serve as organic fertilizer [73].

CQDs obtained through this process are known for their non-toxic nature, good biocompatibility, and widespread use in environmental applications [74,75]. Similarly, hydrochar offers environmental benefits, including pollutant removal from water and atmospheric adsorption of contaminants [76]. It can also contribute to reducing greenhouse gas emissions from soil and improving soil structure [77,78]. By applying these two carbon materials to the AD process, methane production was increased by 20 % and 26 %, respectively. This enhanced methane generation allows for the energy recovery from biogas through cogeneration, potentially offsetting the energy requirements of both the HTC and AD processes. Zhao et al. [72] highlighted that converting AD digestate into hydrothermal charcoal via HTC not only offers environmental benefits but also compensates for the energy used during the process by increasing biogas production. However, it is important to acknowledge that HTC effluent may contain organic matter, inorganic salts, and even potentially toxic compounds [5]. Combining HTC effluent with AD in the future presents a viable solution, as it can reduce inhibitory effects while generating energy, making this integration attractive for both energy production and environmental mitigation.

It is important to note that the initial investment and operational costs for HTC and AD infrastructure, including reactors, heat exchangers, digestion tanks, gas collection systems, and wastewater treatment facilities, are notably high. These costs, however, are expected to decrease through technical optimization and scaled-up production. Further optimization is essential to improve economic efficiency and reduce environmental impacts, contributing to sustainable development. Key factors include the type of feedstock (e.g., different lignin or biomass sources) and the kinetics of carbon material conversion, as well as their impact on methane production. Additionally, a more detailed characterization of the electrochemical properties of the carbon materials is necessary to better understand the relationship between reaction conditions and their performance in microbial electron transfer. Processing CQDs, for instance, currently requires dialysis, which involves considerable initial equipment investment and substantial water use, which may strain water resources and is not suited for large-scale continuous production. An alternative purification method, such as molecular sieve technology, offers a shorter processing time and adapts well to varying production scales [79]. Another issue is the 20 % material loss as incomplete small molecules during HTC and the generation of a large amount of wastewater, which could lead to environmental pollution if not utilized in AD processes. Ideally, all carbonization products and wastewater should be incorporated into the AD reactor, with long-term continuous digestion experiments necessary to assess their applicability and stability in complex systems. LHC and CQDs, combined with digestate, can serve as soil amendments, not only improving the economics of the process but also addressing waste disposal issues of the AD process. Research has demonstrated that HTC-derived materials not only improve soil characteristics and enhance microbial activity but also directly provide essential nutrients to crops [80–82]. For instance, hydrochar has promoted the growth of barley and phaseolus beans [80]. Nonetheless, there are concerns that these materials might adversely affect the growth of soil microorganisms and plants [83–85]. Moreover, there is a lack of standardized protocols for treating anaerobic digestate with additives, and common digestate treatment standards vary across countries such as China, Germany, and the Netherlands [86], necessitating thorough investigations into their effects on soil ecosystems. Finally, current research is predominantly focused on small-scale, batch operations, including HTC and AD processes. Future work should aim to conduct pilot and industrial-scale experiments based on optimized processes. Comprehensive technical evaluations, economic analysis, and life cycle assessments will be crucial to determine the feasibility of scaling these technologies for commercial operations, ensuring that technological advancements are aligned with environmental sustainability goals.

In this study, lignin derived from black liquor has been converted into two different carbon materials, i.e., LHC and CQDs, in a one-pot reaction via HTC. The effects of AL, LHC, and CQDs on the AD process of glucose were systematically investigated. The addition of 5 g/L and 10 g/L of AL resulted in a decline in methane yield, methane production rate, CH4/CO2 ratio, sCOD removal efficiency, and increased lag phase. Conversely, the addition of lignin-based LHC and CQDs exhibited the ability to effectively promote the AD process, improving sCOD removal efficiency and maintaining high coenzyme F420 activity and ATP content. Despite its weak conductivity, LHC was found to enhance the generation of humic acid-like organic compounds, improve the redox capacity of the AD system, and increase methane production by 26.16 % at 3 g/L. In comparison to LHC, CQDs could significantly enhance the conductivity and redox capacity of the AD system. Specifically, the addition of 3 g/L of CQDs resulted in a 17.91 % increase in methane production. The excellent performance of lignin-based carbon materials led to an acceleration in the electron transfer rate between microorganisms and enrichment in hydrolytic acidifying microorganisms, ultimately facilitating the hydrogenotrophic, acetoclastic, and methylotrophic methanogenic pathways. The process proposed in this study provides a promising solution for treating black liquor through waste utilization and energy recovery. However, further research is necessary to optimize the process in terms of technical efficiency, economic viability, and environmental impact.
